back:
Serverless, interactive query service that runs standard SQL directly against data in S3 (via Presto/Trino) without needing to load it anywhere.

Pay per query based on data scanned (per TB), not per hour, so partitioning data and using columnar formats like Parquet/ORC in S3 dramatically cuts cost.

Common exam pattern: ad hoc/one-off analytics on data lake data with no need to manage infrastructure or provision clusters, unlike Redshift or EMR.

Marketplace for finding, subscribing to, and receiving third-party data directly into S3, Redshift, or via API, with billing handled through AWS.

Exam angle: use it when a scenario needs external/third-party datasets (e.g. financial, weather, demographic) delivered and kept up to date without building your own ingestion pipeline from a vendor.

Orchestration service for scheduling and moving/transforming data between AWS compute/storage services and on-premises sources on a recurring schedule.

Largely legacy/deprecated in favor of AWS Glue and Step Functions — on the exam, if a question involves ETL orchestration, Glue or Step Functions is almost always the better/expected answer.

Managed Hadoop/Spark cluster service for big-data processing (Hadoop, Spark, Hive, Presto, HBase), deployable on EC2, EKS, EMR Serverless, or Outposts.

Spot Instances on task nodes are a common cost-optimization choice, since losing a task node doesn't lose data — only core/master nodes hold HDFS data.

Serverless ETL and data-catalog service.

Glue Crawlers infer schema and populate the Glue Data Catalog; Glue Jobs (Spark or Python shell) run the actual data transform.

Runs SQL or Apache Flink applications directly against streaming data from Kinesis Data Streams or Firehose in real time, without needing a separate consumer application.

Used for real-time aggregations, anomaly detection, or windowed computations on a stream — distinguishes from Athena, which queries data at rest, not in motion.

Fully managed delivery service that loads streaming data into S3, Redshift, OpenSearch, or Splunk, with optional Lambda-based transformation and format conversion (e.g. JSON to Parquet) in-flight.

Near-real-time — buffers by size/time, typically seconds to minutes, not true real-time.

Real-time streaming ingestion service partitioned into shards; each shard supports 1MB/sec or 1,000 records/sec write and 2MB/sec read.

Data is retained 24 hours by default (extendable up to 365 days) and can be replayed by multiple independent consumers.

Centralizes and simplifies setting up a secure data lake on S3, layering fine-grained (row/column/tag-based) access control on top of the Glue Data Catalog.

Exam angle: it's the answer whenever a scenario needs centralized permission management across many users/accounts querying the same S3-based data lake via Athena, Redshift Spectrum, or EMR, rather than managing individual S3 bucket policies.

Managed Apache Kafka — AWS runs the brokers and Zookeeper/KRaft control plane, but you still manage topics, partitions, and consumer groups yourself (unlike the fully serverless Kinesis).

Choose MSK over Kinesis Data Streams when a scenario explicitly requires open-source Kafka compatibility, existing Kafka client code, or portability across clouds; MSK Serverless removes capacity planning if you want Kafka semantics without managing brokers.

Managed cluster for the OpenSearch/Elasticsearch engine, used for log analytics, full-text search, and real-time application monitoring (often paired with CloudWatch Logs or Kinesis Data Firehose as a sink).

Exam distinction from Athena/Redshift: OpenSearch is optimized for full-text search and near-real-time log/metric dashboards, not SQL-style structured analytics.

Serverless BI/dashboarding tool with pay-per-session pricing and SPICE, an in-memory columnar cache that speeds up dashboards without hitting the source data store on every view.

Exam angle: it's the visualization/reporting layer on top of Athena, Redshift, RDS, or S3 — not a data processing or storage service itself.

Securely ingests, stores, and indexes streaming video from many devices for playback, ML analysis (e.g. via Rekognition Video), or custom processing — the video-specific counterpart to Kinesis Data Streams, purpose-built for time-indexed media rather than generic records.

front: EMR vs. Athena/Redshift — when do you pick EMR?
back: Pick EMR specifically when you need custom big-data frameworks or existing Hadoop/Spark code — Athena and Redshift are managed-SQL alternatives that don't require bringing your own processing framework.

front: What is the AWS Glue Data Catalog?
back: A central metastore shared by Athena, Redshift Spectrum, and EMR — it's what makes data sitting in S3 queryable as tables across multiple analytics services, without loading it into any of them.

front: Kinesis Data Firehose vs. Kinesis Data Streams — how do you choose?
back:
Choose Data Streams when you need custom consumer logic and sub-second processing latency.

Choose Firehose when you just need to land streaming data into a destination with no consumer code to write or manage.
